summary: MARTA's first-ever bus rapid transit line launches April 18, connecting downtown to the Southside BeltLine through Summerhill and Peoplestown. Plus: Atlanta's tallest new tower in 30 years gets its crane removed in Midtown, the 90th Dogwood Festival returns to Piedmont Park with a new entry fee, and Inman Park welcomes Edibles.com's first retail store steps from the BeltLine.

topStory.headline: MARTA's First Bus Rapid Transit Line Launches April 18, Connecting Downtown to the Southside BeltLine
topStory.summary: The Rapid A-Line is Atlanta's first BRT service, running 5 miles with 14 stops from downtown through Capitol Gateway, Summerhill, and Peoplestown to the BeltLine's Southside Trail. Buses arrive every 10-15 minutes and can change traffic lights to keep moving.
topStory.body:
MARTA is finally entering the bus rapid transit game with the Rapid A-Line launching April 18. Think of it as "rail on rubber tires," they say, and for anyone who's waited 45 minutes for a bus on Capitol Avenue, this is a pretty big deal.

The five-mile route connects downtown Atlanta to Capitol Gateway, Summerhill, Peoplestown, and the BeltLine's Southside Trail with 14 stops. Unlike traditional bus schedules where you're checking an app every two minutes, these buses roll through every 10-15 minutes. They've also got dedicated lanes in many spots and tech that can change traffic lights. Pretty slick.

Now, the catch: this thing cost $123 million, more than double the original budget. MARTA blamed "unexpected infrastructure issues" and a battery recall on their new electric buses. Classic Atlanta infrastructure story. But if it actually runs on time and gets people from Peoplestown to Five Points without sitting in Memorial Drive traffic, most folks won't care about the budget overruns.
topStory.imageAlt: MARTA Rapid A-Line bus rapid transit vehicle on the Southside route

stories.headline: Atlanta's Tallest New Tower in 30 Years Finally Finished as Crane Comes Down
stories.summary: The 60-story 1072 West Peachtree has reached its full height after nearly three years of construction. The crane removal is causing lane closures on West Peachtree between 11th and 12th streets through April 15.
stories.body:
If you've been watching Midtown's skyline evolve, you've seen this tower going up since 2023. Now 1072 West Peachtree is officially done, and the crane that's been a fixture over Midtown is finally coming down.

The 60-story mixed-use building from Rockefeller Group is the tallest new construction Atlanta has seen since the early 1990s. That's not nothing. For context, we're talking about three decades of no one building anything this tall in the city.

The crane disassembly started this week and will continue through Wednesday. If you're driving through Midtown, heads up: all of West Peachtree Street between 11th and 12th streets is closed through April 15, with northbound traffic diverted onto 11th Street. Plan accordingly, especially if you're heading to Piedmont Park for the Dogwood Festival this weekend.
stories.imageAlt: 1072 West Peachtree tower with crane being removed from Midtown skyline

stories.headline: Decatur Launches Curbside Composting Program with CompostNow
stories.summary: The city of Decatur is rolling out a subscription-based curbside composting program starting April 21. For $19/month, residents get a 7-gallon bin and weekly pickup.
stories.body:
Decatur is making it easier to keep food scraps out of the landfill. The city is partnering with CompostNow to launch a curbside residential composting program starting April 21.

The program is subscription-based at $19 per month. Participating households get a 7-gallon curbside compost collection bin. Just toss in your food scraps, and CompostNow handles the rest.

For Decatur residents who've been driving their compost to drop-off sites or maintaining backyard bins, this is a much simpler option. CompostNow already operates in several Atlanta neighborhoods, so the infrastructure is proven. The city joins a growing list of metro Atlanta municipalities making composting more accessible.
stories.imageAlt: CompostNow curbside composting bin for Decatur residents

stories.imageAlt: MARTA Rapid A-Line bus rapid transit vehicle on the Southside route

stories.headline: Georgia Legislature Extends Conservation Program for 10 Years, Boosts Funding 50%
stories.summary: The Georgia Outdoor Stewardship Program, which has allocated $142 million to 74 conservation projects since 2019, gets extended with increased funding. Recent acquisitions include land near the Okefenokee.
stories.body:
Georgia conservationists are celebrating a major win from the legislative session. Lawmakers extended the Georgia Outdoor Stewardship Program for another 10 years and increased its funding by 50%.

Since launching in 2019, the program has allocated over $142 million to 74 conservation and outdoor recreation projects across Georgia. That includes the recent acquisition of nearly 4,000 acres along Trail Ridge near the Okefenokee National Wildlife Refuge.

"When we passed this initially, it was done with a 10-year sunset so that we could prove this was a good, beneficial project," said Mike Worley, head of the Georgia Wildlife Federation. "I think we have clearly proved that through the years." The extension ensures continued funding for land conservation, trail development, and outdoor recreation access statewide.
stories.imageAlt: Georgia State Capitol where conservation funding was approved

stories.headline: 30 Years In, Alon's Bakery Remains a Sandwich Staple in Morningside
stories.summary: The neighborhood institution is still drawing crowds for crusty loaves, stacked sandwiches, and a well-stocked gourmet market. The bresaola panini and smoked peppered turkey on focaccia are must-orders.
stories.body:
With all-day cafes multiplying across Atlanta, Alon's Bakery & Market in Morningside has been doing this since 1992. Founded by namesake owner Alon Balshan, the place still draws crowds for its crusty loaves, stacked sandwiches, and gourmet market.

The move is to skip the croissants (kidding, get the croissants too) and head straight for the sandwiches. The bresaola panini layers paper-thin cured beef, Parmigiano-reggiano, and shaved fennel on ciabatta. The smoked peppered turkey has cult status among regulars, and the move is to order it on focaccia.

For an easy dinner, the prepared foods counter has Moroccan chicken skewers with chimichurri and tri-color couscous. The bakery case turns out some of Atlanta's flakiest croissants, from classic butter to pistachio variations. If you're hosting, the mini pastry basket ($55, serves 12) is a greatest-hits assortment.
stories.imageAlt: Salmon sandwich and pastries at Alon's Bakery in Morningside

stories.headline: Edibles.com Opens First Retail Store in Inman Park, Steps from the BeltLine
stories.summary: The hemp-derived THC company launched its flagship location on the BeltLine near Del Bar and Painted Park. It's positioned as a model for responsible, in-person hemp commerce during ongoing regulatory uncertainty.
stories.body:
Nearly a year after Edibles.com launched online, the company has opened its first brick-and-mortar store in Inman Park. The flagship location sits steps from the BeltLine, surrounded by neighborhood spots like Del Bar and Painted Park.

The Atlanta-based company is trying to do something different in the hemp-derived THC space: build a retail model focused on trust, compliance, and education rather than just moving product. The store features a concierge-style experience with staff trained to answer questions about products and dosing.

Customers must be 21 or older with valid ID. Hours are Tuesday through Saturday from 11 a.m. to 8 p.m. and Sunday from 11 a.m. to 7 p.m. With hemp regulation still a moving target in Georgia, the company is betting that a legitimate, transparent approach will win out over the smoke shop model.
stories.imageAlt: Edibles.com flagship retail store interior in Inman Park near the BeltLine

stories.headline: Downtown's A.J. Robinson to Retire After 23 Years Leading Central Atlanta Progress
stories.summary: Robinson transformed Downtown from 17% office occupancy to a thriving district during his tenure. He'll stay on as volunteer chair of the Atlanta Downtown Improvement District through the World Cup.
stories.body:
A.J. Robinson announced Wednesday he's stepping down from Central Atlanta Progress after 23 years running the Downtown improvement organization. If you've seen Downtown change over the past two decades, Robinson had a hand in it.

"When I took this job, the 191 building was 17 percent occupied," Robinson reflected. "The Macy's building was empty. The Ellis Hotel was still the Winecoff. The Gulch was still the Gulch. There was nothing around Centennial Olympic Park but the Embassy Suites Hotel."

A search committee chaired by developer Egbert Perry will look for Robinson's successor. Robinson expects to stay through summer and will likely continue as volunteer chair of the Atlanta Downtown Improvement District after that. He's also planning to stick around for World Cup preparations. "I'm really not going anywhere," Robinson joked. "This is really a bump back to the future."
stories.imageAlt: A.J. Robinson announces retirement from Central Atlanta Progress

stories.headline: Hypersonic Startup Hermeus Hits $1B Valuation, Moves HQ from Atlanta to Los Angeles
stories.summary: The Atlanta-founded defense aviation company raised $350M in Series C funding and will relocate headquarters to El Segundo. The Atlanta facility remains open for production with no layoffs planned.
stories.body:
Hermeus, the Atlanta-founded company building unmanned hypersonic aircraft for national security missions, just hit unicorn status with a $350 million Series C round. But there's a catch for local tech boosters: they're moving headquarters to El Segundo, right next to LAX.

The company will keep its Atlanta facility open for production, with no layoffs planned and active hiring for engineering talent. But the HQ move is a loss for Atlanta's aerospace ecosystem, even if the production jobs stay.

Hermeus works with the Department of Defense and has investors including NASA, the U.S. Air Force, and Sam Altman. The team has grown to more than 275 employees across Atlanta, Los Angeles, DC, and Jacksonville. "Speed is life for us," said CEO AJ Piplica. "This new funding lets us build multiple aircraft at the same time and scale our manufacturing capabilities."
stories.imageAlt: Hermeus hypersonic aircraft development in Atlanta

stories.headline: Oasis Garden Bringing Outdoor Social Dining Experience to Atlanta This Spring
stories.summary: The new outdoor restaurant at 3250 E. Shadowlawn Ave. will seat 160 with a design inspired by summertime in the city. Menu focuses on barbecue-inspired dishes.
stories.body:
A new outdoor dining destination is headed to Atlanta. Oasis Garden is opening this spring at 3250 E. Shadowlawn Ave. NE with a 3,790-square-foot space designed for 160 guests.

The concept promises "a polished outdoor escape with vibrant energy, intentional hospitality and social dining made for unforgettable nights," according to the team. Think summertime-in-the-city energy with black-and-white stripes, birds of paradise, and an upscale casual vibe.

The menu will focus on barbecue-inspired dishes, which should pair nicely with the outdoor setting. It's another addition to Atlanta's growing roster of patios and rooftops that lean into the city's long warm-weather season.
stories.imageAlt: Oasis Garden outdoor restaurant concept rendering

stories.headline: Space Explorers: The Infinite Brings Virtual ISS Views to Pullman Yards
stories.summary: The immersive VR exhibition lets visitors experience views from the International Space Station. It's currently on display at Pullman Yards in Kirkwood.
stories.body:
If you've ever wondered what it's like to float through the International Space Station, Space Explorers: The Infinite at Pullman Yards is as close as most of us will ever get.

The immersive exhibition uses virtual reality to put you inside the ISS, with footage captured by astronauts during actual missions. You walk through recreated modules while wearing VR headsets that show you what the crew sees: Earth spinning below, the cramped quarters, the everyday routines of life in space.

Pullman Yards, the historic rail facility turned event space in Kirkwood, has become Atlanta's go-to venue for these kinds of immersive experiences. The Space Explorers exhibition adds to a lineup that's included everything from Van Gogh projections to Stranger Things activations.
stories.imageAlt: Space Explorers: The Infinite VR experience at Pullman Yards

stories.headline: Fulton's Reparations Task Force Delivers 615-Page Report on Harm Done to Black Residents
stories.summary: The comprehensive report documents historical harms from slavery through urban renewal and recommends policy changes in education, housing, and criminal justice. Fulton is one of the largest counties nationally to produce such a study.
stories.body:
Fulton County's Reparations Task Force has released a 615-page report documenting how government policies at every level systematically disadvantaged Black Atlantans, from slavery through Jim Crow, urban renewal, and into the present.

The report examines discriminatory housing practices, unequal school funding, and the targeted destruction of thriving Black neighborhoods. Think about what urban renewal did to Sweet Auburn and Buttermilk Bottom. That history is in here, along with the ongoing effects.

Recommendations span education, housing, economic development, and criminal justice reform, including targeted investments in historically Black neighborhoods and expanded homeownership programs. Fulton joins California and Evanston (Illinois) among jurisdictions that have produced formal reparations studies. The Board of Commissioners will review findings in coming weeks, with community input sessions expected before any votes.
stories.imageAlt: Fulton County Reparations Task Force report release

stories.headline: Black Midwife Sues Georgia Over Birth Laws She Says Harm Black Mothers
stories.summary: Licensed midwife Jamarah Amani argues Georgia's restrictions on non-nurse midwives and physician supervision requirements limit access to care. Georgia has some of the most restrictive midwife policies in the nation.
stories.body:
Jamarah Amani knows what it's like to fight with hospital staff during labor. After a harrowing experience with her second child, where a nurse told her that her birthing choices would "kill the baby," she became a midwife to help others avoid the same treatment.

Now Amani, co-founder of the National Black Midwives Alliance, is suing Georgia over laws she says restrict access to midwifery care. Georgia doesn't allow midwives without a nursing degree to practice, and those with nursing degrees must work under physician supervision.

Advocates call Georgia's midwife policies among the most restrictive in the nation, at a time when the state's maternal mortality rates (particularly for Black mothers) remain among the worst in the country. Amani had to move to Florida in 2008 just to pursue her career. "No one should have to fight when they're in labor," she said.
stories.imageAlt: Jamarah Amani, licensed midwife and co-founder of National Black Midwives Alliance

stories.headline: FBI, Atlanta Police Warn Parents About '764' Extremist Network Targeting Kids Online
stories.summary: The violent nihilistic group lures children as young as 9 on private platforms, then uses intimidation to coerce them into self-harm, animal cruelty, or violence. Atlanta police have opened five related investigations.
stories.body:
The FBI and Atlanta Police are sounding the alarm about an extremist network called '764' that's targeting Georgia children through online platforms. The group has been linked to several local cases, including bomb threats called in by minors.

"The group lures children, in some cases as young as 9 years old, to private platforms and then grooms them through intimidation and abuse," said Atlanta Police Chief Darin Schierbaum.

Once contact is made, 764 members coerce victims into producing extreme content, self-harm, animal cruelty, or violence against others. Atlanta PD has opened five investigations they believe are connected to the network, including terrorist threats made by kids ages 9 to 15. The FBI described it as "a form of violence, motivated by a hatred of society and a drive to cause collapse through indiscriminate chaos and destruction."
stories.imageAlt: FBI and Atlanta Police press conference about 764 extremist network

stories.headline: Two GSP Troopers Hospitalized After Range Rover Chase, Crash in Buckhead
stories.summary: A driver fled a traffic stop on Peachtree Road, ran a red light at Piedmont Road, crashed into another car, and ran. Two troopers were injured during the foot pursuit before the suspect was Tased and captured.
stories.body:
Two Georgia State Patrol troopers ended up in the hospital Thursday night after a chase through Buckhead went sideways.

Troopers tried pulling over a Range Rover on Peachtree Road around 9:30 p.m. Instead of stopping, the driver took off, ran a red at Piedmont Road, and slammed into another car. Then they jumped out and ran.

During the foot pursuit, two troopers suffered minor injuries. Another trooper Tased the suspect and made the arrest. The other driver in the crash had non-life-threatening injuries but declined to go to the hospital. GSP hasn't released the suspect's name or said what sparked the original traffic stop. The crash shut down Piedmont Road for about an hour.
stories.imageAlt: Georgia State Patrol response to Buckhead chase and crash

stories.headline: Atlanta Tech Hub Hits 3-Year Mark with $37 Million Mobilized, 1,155 New Hires
stories.summary: Mayor's Office of Technology and Innovation released its 2025 Impact Report showing the tech hub has supported 146 companies, generated $70 million in salaries, and committed $85 million in city pension capital to VC and private equity.
stories.body:
Three years into its mission to make Atlanta a top-five tech hub, the Mayor's Office of Technology and Innovation is touting some numbers. The Atlanta Tech Hub released its 2025 Impact Report at an event complete with Hamilton costumes and movie stars.

Since launching, the hub has mobilized over $37 million in investments, supported companies adding 1,155 new hires, and generated nearly $70 million in salaries. All with a team of four people. The city has committed $85 million in pension capital to private equity and venture capital.

"Atlanta has all the ingredients on the table to bake a really great cake, and the 2025 Impact Report shows that our ecosystem's flywheel is officially spinning," said Donnie Beamer, the Tech Hub head. The report predicts 92% of supported companies will hire more in the next 12 months.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta Tech Hub 2025 Impact Report presentation
